claimed_contribution: The paper shows that even when consumers make a purchase, the same option can produce lower satisfaction when it is believed to come from a larger rather than a smaller assortment.

It identifies expectation disconfirmation as the mechanism: larger assortments raise expectations of preference match, which leads to more negative or less positive disconfirmation and lower satisfaction.
It provides evidence that this expectation-based mechanism operates independently of, and in addition to, overload effects, and can emerge when regret is minimized.
context: The research studies consumer choice from assortments in three product domains: birthday cards, camcorders, and computer wallpapers. The settings include principal-agent choices for a coworker and self-choice with either full catalog exposure or free search across small versus large assortments.
design_and_data: The paper reports three between-subjects experiments with undergraduate samples (Study 1: N=120, Study 2: N=165, Study 3: N=65). Study 1 manipulated perceived set size (10 vs 100 cards) while participants evaluated the same chosen card, measured expectations, disconfirmation, and satisfaction, and used mediation tests. Study 2 used a 2 x 2 design (assortment size 8 vs 32 camcorders x question order) with full set exposure, measured subjective and objective overload plus disconfirmation and satisfaction, and tested parallel mediation. Study 3 manipulated assortment size (60 vs 300 wallpapers) in a free-search self-choice task where the small set was a random subset of the large set, recorded search behavior, and tested whether disconfirmation mediated size effects on satisfaction beyond overload.
main_findings: Larger assortments increased consumers' expectations about how well they could match their preferences.

Consumers reported lower satisfaction with chosen options from larger versus smaller assortments, including when evaluating the same option and when larger sets offered objectively better matching opportunities.

This satisfaction drop was mediated by expectation disconfirmation (greater negative disconfirmation in Study 1, less positive disconfirmation in Studies 2-3), and in Study 2 overload also affected satisfaction but did not eliminate the independent role of disconfirmation.
one_line_summary: Across three experiments, larger assortments reduced satisfaction with chosen options because they elevated preference-match expectations and generated less favorable expectation disconfirmation, beyond overload effects.
relation_to_prior_work: extends prior research on large-assortment downsides beyond choice deferral, switching, and regret by showing an expectation-disconfirmation pathway that lowers postchoice satisfaction and operates in parallel with overload.
research_gap: Prior assortment-size research had mainly documented effects on choice deferral, switching, overload, and regret, but did not clearly explain whether larger assortments lower satisfaction after a choice is made. The paper addresses this by testing an explicit expectation-disconfirmation account and separating it from overload.
research_question: Do larger assortments reduce satisfaction with a chosen option even when consumers complete a purchase? If so, is the effect driven by higher expectations of preference match that create greater negative or less positive expectation disconfirmation, rather than only by overload?
